comments.text: (This comment has been pinned.) This problem has now had two full solution claims posted by Chojecki and MalekZ, both using an earlier sketch developed here in comments mainly due to Sawhney and Tao. I think this is sufficient advertisement for those claims, and will not be allowing new comments along similar lines (using AI to generate a detailed proof from the Sawhney-Tao sketch). Given the subtleties and technicalities involved, I will await either publication of a solution in a peer-reviewed journal, or a careful look from an expert human (e.g. Sawhney or Tao), before updating the site. (Or at some point I may try to read/think about this carefully myself, but not sure when I will have the time.) Until then, please hold further discussion of these AI-assisted proofs outside this forum. (As ever, new quality human comments are always welcome.)

Here's my attempted solution revolving on the reduction of problem to a finite-core prime-difference matching I simply need some input on the construction of the Green–Tao’s codimension-≤2 affine-linear prime theorem, plus Kahn’s fractional matching theorem. This version includes an explicit local-factor convention and moment audit. I appreciate any help on this, and whether or not this fully closes the problem after a good check. I used 5.5 to tidy up some portions of the work. This proof builds off of Premzek's previous note along side my own prior work on this problem. PDF

This is a full solution candidate. (Please say so - "attempted solution" could be anything including just vague proposed strategy.) The provenance here is a bit complicated. Apart from Chojecki, it appears previous discussions by Tao, Sawhney, and others are direct precursors of what's in the paper. I'm just stating this here so we know what to map. The paper has complexity which makes simple checking less reliable in my opinion. Nevertheless, a standard check is run in this discussion and no issue has been found. So this is at least a serious solution candidate.

I completely understand, and for the record I had no intention of vague posting, rather I didn't want to over claim the contents of the paper, and thought that leaving the complete verdict of the paper up to someone more credentialed would be better. Either way, I appreciate and thank you the comment and the check.

Yes, I know. Actually, I also know that recent discussions of overclaiming AI results can and do push people towards being conservative to try to be safe. But consider, say, your interactions with AI. Clearly, you don't want it claiming everything with overconfidence. But: if it starts to hedge on every position out of fear of judgment (some recent models do tend toward this!), this can also be equally or more annoying, and usefulness can even degrade by this. What you really want is something more like: be assertive but honest about limitations. So I suppose there's some common lesson about "optimization pressure" regarding both cases...

Ok, I'm glad we're on the same page here hah. I'll continue to drill the models regarding the actual verdict of work. To make it completely clear: I am actually capable of determining the verdict of work myself, which I do. I just feel like a neutral source (5.5 pro) is able to give others a cleaner sense of validation due to the fact of my lack of public credentials. Thanks

Typo: the interval $[1,n]$ should be open ended on the right side. This is how the question is posed in [Er80]. Without this, It is not obvious how to make every integer in the interval satisfy even one of the congruences, because we can no longer rely entirely on homogeneous congruences to do the job.

I've done many back-and-forths with GPT-5.5 Pro on this problem and basically the solution converges to some variant of use Green-Tao-Ziegler + hypergraphs, which seems similar to what Malek posted below as well. The problem is some computations are pretty delicate, so I'm posting this note more as a working manuscript and would love to get experts' opinions on this one.

I’ve rewritten the proposed proof as a verification package, with the deterministic debt/matching steps separated from the analytic input. The only point I’m asking people to check now is whether the weighted GTZ moment proposition in Appendix A really follows from the finite-complexity linear-equations-in-primes theorem. In particular, are the four local-factor identities for the edge, Z-, X-, and Y-second-moment systems correct? AI tools helped with the exposition/checking, but I have personally read the note and verified it to my highest degree, simply asking for whats left (if anything) for a verified solution. Thanks in advance to anyone who decides to review. PDF note here

I have a proposed proof of Problem 689 for all sufficiently large \(n\). A short PDF note is here: PDF note **Disclosure:** this write-up was prepared with AI assistance in finding literature and reviewing claims. I am posting it as a proposed proof and request for verification, not as a refereed result. This is meant as an attempt to make precise the route suggested in the earlier comments: use linear equations in primes to control averaged prime-pattern counts, and use hypergraph matching/nibble technology to turn the resulting fractional cover into a genuine disjoint cover. The new ingredient in the note is the robust \(P>n/5\) cleanup setup and the finite-core fractional matching formulation. The strategy is as follows. Start with \(a_2\equiv 1\pmod 2\), leave \(3\) in the zero class, and switch a fixed finite set\[ S\subset\{7,11,13,\ldots\} \]to nonzero residues. After these fixed switches, the main residual demand consists of even numbers\[ x=2^k u q,\qquad k\ge1, \]where \(u\) is odd \(S\)-smooth and \(q
otin S\) is prime, subject to fixed congruence exclusions. Fixed-modulus PNT gives\[ |A_S(n)|=(1+o(1)) rac n{\log n}, \]with half of this mass at \(v_2(x)=1\) and half at \(v_2(x)\ge2\). The cleanup primes are primes \(P>n/5\). Call \(P\) robust if\[ H_S(P)\ge1,\qquad H_S(2P)\ge2,\qquad H_S(4P)\ge2, \]where \(H_S(x)\) counts the switched \(S\)-classes hitting \(x\). Switching a robust \(P\) creates no new unresolved debt: the only multiples \(P,2P,3P,4P\le n\) are covered by robustness plus parity and the unchanged \(0\pmod3\) class. By choosing \(S\) large enough, the robust residue density \(\delta_S\) can be made \(>0.94388\ldots\). Choose\[ \delta_S^{-1}-3/5< eta<	frac12(1-	frac35 e^{-2}), \]and set \(\Delta=( eta+3/5)\delta_S-1>0\) for the surplus margin used below. Build a 3-partite hypergraph with vertex classes\[ X=X_n\subset A_1(n),\qquad Y=Y_n\subset A_2(n),\qquad Z=\{P\in(n/5, eta n]:P	ext{ robust}\}, \]where \(X_n,Y_n\) are finite coefficient cores capturing fractions \(\alpha_X,\alpha_Y>G( eta)+\eta\) of the \(A_1,A_2\) coefficient mass, and edges\[ (x,y,P)\quad	ext{when}\quad |y-x|=2P. \]A matching covering \((1-o(1))|Z|\) labels gives enough paired covers; the remaining residual targets, including the coefficient tails outside the finite cores and the \(o(n/\log n)\) exceptional residual tokens, are covered singly by unused robust primes. The strict surplus \(\Delta>0\) is exactly what leaves enough unused robust primes for the singleton cleanup once the cores are chosen so that the discarded coefficient-tail mass plus exceptional tokens is at most \(\Delta N/10\). The matching is obtained by a finite-core fractional construction. In half-residue coordinates \(A\equiv aq\pmod W\), \(B\equiv bq'\pmod W\), the residual classes are a product set \(\mathcal C\), and for every unit label residue \(\pi\),\[ \#\{A\in\mathcal C:A\pm\pi\in\mathcal C\}=\prod_{s\in S}(s-2). \]This gives an explicit continuum transport kernel with exact label load \(1\) and side load bounded by\[ G( eta)=\int_{1/5}^{ eta} rac{dt}{1-2t}<1. \]Finite coefficient cores scale this by the captured core mass, giving side bounds \(G( eta)/\alpha_X\) and \(G( eta)/\alpha_Y\), both strictly less than \(1\) by the choice of cores. The aggregate transport then lifts to bounded typed kernels on the finitely many admissible typed polygons, with limiting label load \(1\) and bounded side loads. The analytic input is the finite-complexity Green-Tao-Ziegler theorem for affine-linear forms in primes. In a detailed proof this can be formulated using an auxiliary growing \(W_{\mathrm{GTZ}}\)-trick while keeping the fixed modulus \(W=\prod_{s\in S}s\) as residue data; equivalently, one can work with fixed-modulus singular series and verify the corresponding local-factor disintegrations in the first and second moment systems. It supplies the first and second weighted moment estimates for the edge loads. Importantly, no pointwise Hardy-Littlewood / Bateman-Horn estimate for fixed \(P=bq'-aq\) is used; all estimates are averaged finite-complexity linear-form counts. The deletion step uses the standard \(L^2\)-to-mass-loss argument: vertices with normalized side load above \(1\) have \(|L_X(x)-L_X^{\mathrm{lim}}(x)|\ge 2\gamma\), so the side \(L^2\) estimate forces \(|B_X|=o(|X_n|)\), and a Cauchy-Schwarz finish bounds the deleted mass by \(o(|Z_n|)\) on each side. The final rounding input is Kahn's fractional Frankl-Rödl-Pippenger theorem (Random Structures and Algorithms 8 (1996), 149-157), applied with the single statistic \(C(e)=1\). The hypergraph has codegree at most \(2\): a pair \((x,y)\) determines \(P=|y-x|/2\), and a pair \((x,P)\) or \((y,P)\) has at most two extensions. Hence\[ a(t)=\max_{u
e v}\sum_{e\supset\{u,v\}}t_e\le 2\max_e t_e=o(1). \]**What I would especially appreciate help with.** The two interfaces I would most like checked are: (1) **Kahn 1996, Theorem 1.5.** I have not been able to access the printed paper directly. Public metadata/abstracts for Kahn's paper confirm the pair co-load parameter\[ \alpha(t)=\max\Big\{\sum\{t(A): x,y\in A\in\mathcal H\}:x,y\in V,\ x
e y\Big\} \]exactly as I use it. What I cannot verify from public sources is the precise form of the conclusion in the non-perfect case: specifically, that for a fractional matching \(t\) with total mass \(\sum_e t_e=(1-o(1))|Z_n|\), \(\max_e t_e=o(1)\), and \(\alpha(t)=o(1)\), Theorem 1.5 produces an integral matching \(M\) with \(|M|=(1-o(1))|Z_n|\). If anyone with access to the printed paper can confirm that this is the right shape of the conclusion, or flag a hypothesis I have missed, I would be very grateful. (2) **The GTZ moment formulation.** I use one edge-total system and three second-moment systems on a fixed finite coefficient core. The note identifies the linear forms used in each system and verifies that no two are rationally affinely dependent after diagonal removal. The local-factor disintegration of the second-moment main terms, under either an auxiliary \(W_{\mathrm{GTZ}}\)-trick or fixed-modulus singular series, is asserted rather than written out in full. I would welcome any flag if this is not the right shape, or if the systems require additional admissibility checks I have missed. I do not currently see a hidden pointwise prime-pair input, no Hardy-Littlewood, Bateman-Horn, Elliott-Halberstam, or Goldbach-type pointwise estimate is used in the argument as written though I would welcome being shown otherwise.

Was your writeup describing your pdf note also generated with AI use? The AI's strategy seems interesting, but I wouldn't classify it as a proof. It is at best a sketch or a description of a strategy that could lead to a proof.

Partially, both forum summary and PDF note were read over by Codex, I had taken its changes and applied them, as disclosed. I completely agree that it shouldn't be perceived as a verified proof at this stage. “Proposed proof sketch / verification request” is probably the most accurate description of the current state. The intended content is a reduction/proof stack rather than a fully polished manuscript: robust-prime cleanup, finite-core typed kernel, GTZ averaged moment estimates, Kahn fractional rounding, and the final singleton cleanup ledger. The two interfaces I most want checked are exactly the GTZ moment formulation and the Kahn rounding step. If your objection is that the note does not yet expand those standard-theorem applications enough to be a complete write-up, I agree. I am working on expanded appendices for those parts. If you see a specific mathematical gap where the strategy cannot be made into a proof, I would be very grateful if you could point to it. Thanks for your comment

Updated PDF with Kahn's Theorem 1.5 verified directly against the printed paper, the typed-kernel lift written out with explicit load identities, and the GTZ admissibility and local-factor identities checked in line: pdf (prepared with AI assistance, as before, specifically from Claude and codex). I'd very much appreciate a careful read of the second-moment systems (22)–(24) and the local-factor identities (26), as these are the steps where independent eyes would matter most.
Progress on this problem may also help resolve [1139].

In [1139] I've added a missing argument that would probably also solve this problem. This is a shortened version on the core from my discussions with GPT-5.2: If one could carry out the 2-fold covering construction in its “strong form” --- namely, for each sufficiently large $n$ choose residue classes $a_p \pmod p$ for \emph{every} prime $2\le p\le n$ so that each $j\in[1,n]$ lies in at least two of these classes --- then taking $N$ by CRT with $N\equiv -a_p\pmod p$ for all $p\le n$ would force every $N+j$ ($1\le j\le n$) to be divisible by at least two distinct primes $\le n$. Since the CRT modulus $\prod_{p\le n}p$ is astronomically large, we have $N\gg n^2$, so $N+j$ cannot equal a product of just those two primes; it must have an additional factor, hence $\Omega(N+j)\ge 3$ for all $j\le n$. In other words, this 2-fold congruence cover would directly produce length-$n$ intervals with no primes or semiprimes, giving a very strong form of the “$P_2$-free gaps” phenomenon. Conversely, the strategy we discussed for large gaps between consecutive $\Omega\le2$ numbers is essentially an attempt to build such a 2-fold cover, but it allows two relaxations: (i) it may use only a carefully chosen subset of primes up to $n$ (to keep $\log N=\sum\log p$ small enough to make $n/\log N$ large), and (ii) it may leave a tiny exceptional set of “stragglers” handled separately. If one succeeds even with these relaxations, one gets $\limsup (u_{k+1}-u_k)/\log k=\infty$. If one succeeds in the stronger “use all primes $p\le n$ and cover everything twice” form, then it also answers your finite question outright (and in fact implies a stronger interval construction than what is needed for the $\limsup$).
Just to keep note, this question with 2 replaced by 10 is Problem #45 in Ben Green's open problem list. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

Here are a few more details on the numerology that suggests that a construction is possible. Let $z$ be a sufficiently large constant, and let $A$ be a parameter growing slowly with $z$, e.g., $A = \exp( (\log\log\log z)^2 )$ should work. Assume $n$ extremely large depending on $z$. We hold the primes $n/2 < p \leq n$ in reserve to clean up any stragglers; if we can sieve $\{1,\dots,n\}$ down to less than $n / 4 \log n$ such stragglers then we win. Accordingly, any set of cardinality $o(n/\log n)$ we view as negligible. We first sieve out $0 \pmod{p}$ for all $p \leq n/z$. Up to negligible sets, this leads the primes $n/z < p_1 \leq n$ (which are completely untouched), as well as semiprimes $p_1 p_2$ where $p_1 < z$ and $n/z <p_2 \leq n/p_1$ and $p_1 < z$ (which were touched once). (There are also a few elements of the form $p_1^j p_2$ but these are unlikely to cause trouble). There are about $n/\log n$ primes in the first category and $n \log\log z / \log n$ in the second category. To eliminate most of the primes in the first category, as well as semiprimes $p_1 p_2$ in the second category with $p_1 > z^{1/A}$ (of which there are about $n \log A / \log n$), we use the primes $p \geq n/z^{1/A}$. A congruence class mod such a prime hits about $n/p$ elements in $\{1,\dots,n\}$, of which about $n/(p \log (n/p))$ are usable for this purpose (one has to sieve out multiples of any primes less than $n/p$). We have $\sum_{n/z^{1/A} \leq p \leq z} n/(p \log (n/p)) \sim n \log\log(z^{1/A}) / \log n$ which is somewhat larger than the number $n \log A/\log n$ of targets, so a random sieve here should work well using the linear equations in primes technology (one has to design the random construction so that all primes and semiprimes have a decent chance of being hit, but this should be standard albeit tedious calculations). To touch the semiprimes $p_1 p_2$ with $p_1 \leq z^{1/A}$ we use the primes $n/z \leq p \leq n/z^{10/A}$. A congruence associated to such a prime $p$ can touch about $n/(p p_1 \log(n/p))$ semiprimes $p_1 p_2$ attached to $p_1$; there are about $n / p_1 \log n$ such primes, and the sum of the $n/(p p_1 \log(n/p))$ is about $n \log A / p_1 \log n$, so we (barely) win here (random constructions should then cut down the number of survivors by a factor of $A$ or so, which brings us to within range of being cleaned up by the reserve primes). Again one needs linear equations in primes technology to show that most targets get a reasonable chance of being touched at least once. I *think* something similar can work for larger $r$ than $r=2$, although one has to split into more cases than the two outlined above. If anyone is interested in developing these ideas into a full proof, I'd be happy to talk further.

Mehtaab has pointed out that even for $r=3$ one cannot hope to get a similar argument going with $z$ bounded independently of $n$, since sieving up to $n/z$ now produces about $n \log\log n / \log n$ surviving semiprimes $p_1 p_2$ with $z < p_1, p_2 < n/z$ and each prime $p>n/z$ can only hope to knock out $O(n/(p \log(n/p)))$ of them, which sums to $n \log\log z / \log n$ which is too small. One could try increasing $z$ significantly to get around this obstacle, but once $z$ gets above $\log n$ or so it becomes extremely unrealistic to hope that one can find residue classes that consistently hit a near-maximum fraction of targets. So I am now leaning towards the $r \geq 3$ version of this claim actually being false. (From standard sieves one can show that no matter what congruences one picks, sieving up to $n^{1/2-\varepsilon}$ will leave $\gg n (\log\log n)^{r-1} / \log n$ survivors, which looks challenging to eradicate with the remaining congruences, requiring an unreasonably high level of efficiency for quite long arithmetic progressions.)

This is a really minor remark in the direction of formalizing the obstruction which Terry points out. We first sieve all the primes $\le n^{1/2-\varepsilon}$; this leaves $\gg n(\log\log n)^{r-1}/\log n$ survivors. We now consider the efficiency of primes between $n/\exp((\log n)^{o(1)})$ and $n$. Note that for a fixed prime $p$ we have $n/p$ targets. Furthermore these $n/p$ targets should satisfy at most $r-2$ of the residue class conditions less than $n/p$; the set of integers which satisfy $r-1$ targets below $n/p$ are an atypical subset of those which survive until $\le n^{1/2-\varepsilon}$. The total contribution therefore from primes between $n/\exp((\log n)^{o(1)})$ and $n$ is $\ll \sum_{n/\exp((\log n)^{o(1)})\le p \le n}(n/p)\log\log(n/p)^{r-2}/\log(n/p) = o(n/\log n \cdot (\log\log n)^{r-1})$ if I computed correctly. (We are essentially sieving an AP of length $z$ by primes less than $z$ so we get an accurate upper bound.) The question of course now is the primes between $n^{1/2-\varepsilon}$ and $n/\exp((\log n)^{o(1)})$; the upper bounds above seems really inaccurate as $n/p$ is much much bigger than $\log n$ so a random model would suggest out upper bound is far from sharp.

I tried to see whether any of these arguments can provide *upper* bounds on how large $r$ can be for a given $n$. Just from the fact that $\sum_{p \leq n} n/p \sim n \log\log n$ and the pigeonhole principle it is clear that $r$ cannot exceed $(1+o(1))\log\log n$. But, to my surprise, I cannot improve upon this trivial bound at all! As far as I can tell, it is actually completely consistent with sieve theory - albeit extremely unlikely - that one can cover every number from $1$ to $n$ about $\log\log n$ times using one residue class for each prime up to $n$.

A very trivial remark: I didn't check if this was stated explicitly by Erdos, but presumably the intention was to assume that $n$ is sufficiently large, since the claim is obviously false for, e.g., $n=2$. Also, for what it is worth, ChatGPT did not find any additional literature on this problem. Gemini Deep Research was much wordier (and made much hay out of inexplicably not being able to locate Erdos problem #688), but ultimately did not also find any new references on this specific problem.

This problem appears on page 79 of the source [Er79d,p.79] available online here. The full containing paragraph is: "Recently, I considered the following modification of the above problem . Denote by $\varepsilon_n$ the smallest number so that there is a residue $b_p$ for every prime $p$ with $n^{\varepsilon_n} < p \leqq n$, and every positive integer $x \leqq n$ satisfies at least one of the congruences $x \equiv b_p \pmod{p}$. Is it true that $\varepsilon_n \to 0$ as $n\to\infty$? I can prove that $\varepsilon_n > c \log \log \log n/\log \log n$. Are there residues $c_p$ for every prime $p$ with $2 \leqq p \leqq n$ so that every positive integer $x \leqq n$ satisfies at least $2$ (or at least $r$) of the congruences $x \equiv c_p \pmod{p}$?" (The first problem stated is [688], which also corrects the word "smallest" to "maximal".) In particular, there is no qualification at all on $n$. I would interpret the implied question as asking at least "(for each $r$,) is it possible for some $n$?" as well as "is it possible for all sufficiently large $n$?" but others may differ in their interpretations.
Agreed. "Is It possible for all sufficiently large $n$ in terms of $r$" is my interpretation, indeed.

Sieving out $0 \pmod{p}$ for all $p \leq n/z$ for a medium sized $z$ will leave about $O(n \log\log z/\log n)$ survivors, with $\sim n/\log n$ congruences left to allocate, which could optimistically cover $\sum_{n/z \leq p \leq n} \frac{n}{p} \sim n \log z / \log n$ of the survivors, which would be more than sufficient once $z$ is moderately large. There is a decent chance that modern "large gaps between primes" technology, such as in this paper of Maynard, this paper of Ford-Green-Konyagin-T., or Ford-Green-Konyagin-Maynard-T. might then close off the problem, but I'll leave this for others to work out.

My suspicion is the above commenter (?) reached this point in their analysis as they obviously are rather more knowledgeable here... It seemed worth explaining why following to the nose the older argument of Rankin was insufficient; we follow notes of Ford (https://ford126.web.illinois.edu/lectnotes_2025.pdf; Section 2.2). The change to compute is what happens following the first part of the analysis here; we set $y=x$ as this is the case of interest. There are now instead $\approx x(\log z)/(\log x \log\log x)$ integers which remain after the initial sieving stage. The contribution from integers which are not hit at all after the first stage is still only $\lesssim x/(\log x)$ and these are easily mopped up via the second and third part of the analysis (the second part using random sieving removes almost all such integers and then we can pick of the small remainder one by one by using half to the large primes). The issue is after the first stage there are many integers of the form $pq$ with $p$ a prime between $x/z$ and $x/2$ and $q$ an integers which is coprime to all primes less than $2\log x$. The random sieving now reduces one to a subset of size $x/(\log x)$ essentially exactly; using $1$ prime at a time now becomes too expensive (and this to my understanding is where the previous generation large gaps technology was blocked). The more recent techniques in the aforementioned papers should allows one to hit 1000 primes at a time on average which would complete the proof. It is curious that the question is so delicate (and seems to magically detect the long gaps question).

Yes, that was basically the back of the envelope calculations I was using. In some ways the problem here has a simpler numerology than the long gaps one, and one can see the potential for a win here a bit more easily as follows. I tend to visualize these covering congruence problems as that of knocking down a progression of toy ducks at a carnival shooting gallery: each prime $p \leq n$ represents a "spraying bullet" in one's gun that can hit all the ducks that lie in one congruence class mod $p$. In this particular "game", each duck requires two "hits" to get knocked down, and the objective is to shoot in such a way that all ducks get knocked down. (I apologize for the violent nature of this metaphor; if someone can propose a more neutral one, I'll be happy to switch over to it.) Suppose one "shoots" at the congruence classes $0 \pmod{p}$ for all $p \leq n/2$. Then almost all the ducks are already hit twice by these congruences; the only remaining ducks (that received only zero or one hits) are primes, or powers of primes - so about $n / \log n$ ducks in all. On the other hand, we have about $n/2\log n$ congruences left in reserve (coming from primes $n/2 < p \leq n$) to position judiciously. Each congruence can be used to hit any specific duck once, so this can knock out about $n/4\log n$ of the remaining $n/\log n$ ducks: not enough to win, but tantalizingly close. If, as you say, one could use each of the large primes $p$ to hit many ducks simultaneously, we would have a good chance of actually winning. Unfortunately, each prime $p > n/2$ can only hit at most two ducks in $\{1,\dots,n\}$, and in fact probably only one in practice since almost all of the remaining ducks are odd. So this doesn't directly work, which is why I suggested the modified strategy in which one shoots out $0 \pmod{p}$ for $p \leq n/z$, leaving about $n \log\log z / \log n$ ducks remaining, but now one has as remaining ammunition some primes as small as $n/z$, each of which could potentially hit up to $z$ targets at once. If one could indeed hit the maximum number $n/p$ of ducks with each such shot then we would be in very good shape as indicated above since $n \log z / \log n$ can be made significantly larger than $n \log\log z/\log n$. Unfortunately one cannot quite do this well because many of the ducks are not divisible by small primes, in particular a prime $p \sim n/z$ can only hit about $O(z/\log z)$ $z$-rough targets by standard sieve theory. If one went with this bound then the total number of hits one can hope to make is $\sum_{n/z \leq p \leq z} \frac{n}{p \log(n/p)} \sim n \log\log z /\log n$ so one is now in a similar situation to before (off by a factor of 2 rather than 4, but still not enough). But on the other hand, only $n/\log n$ or so of the $n \log\log z/\log n$ remaining ducks are actually $z$-rough. I did some back of the envelope estimates of the number of "ducks" at different levels of roughness, and what each remaining "bullet" can hope to "hit" in each category, and I think there is actually enough "ammunition" to hit every duck twice. In the modern long gaps literature, there are three basic tools to try to hit a lot of primes with progressions as efficiently as possible: the Maynard sieve; the linear equations in primes theory of Green, Ziegler, and myself; and the hypergraph covering lemmas of Pippenger-Spencer (and later authors). Here I think the Maynard sieve will not be sufficient as it only picks up about $\log z$ primes in a length $z$ progression. This leaves the linear equations in primes theory, which as you know has significantly worse quantitative dependence on $z$ (even with your recent advances), but for this problem I think we can take $z$ to be a large absolute constant and $n$ extremely large (if we only care about the asymptotic version of the problem) and so the error terms in the linear equations in primes results should not be too important. (As a first approximation we can try to establish the result conditionally on the prime tuples conjecture; the linear equations in primes theory roughly speaking asserts that this conjecture holds for almost all shift parameters, which should be sufficient.) The hypergraph covering tools (using the "Rodl nibble") offer some efficiencies in making sure that different "bullets" shoot mostly disjoint sets, but there is enough room in the numerology that I think a simpler probabilistic construction will probably suffice, but one can certainly keep this tool in one's pocket in case the analysis gets too delicate. Anyway, would be happy to discuss this further on this forum or elsewhere, in case there is interest in carrying this argument to the finish line. (In principle one might be able to bash this out with heavy AI assistance, but I think I'd actually prefer a human collaboration (perhaps in a Polymath style) for this one.) p.s. I am not so surprised that this problem of Erdos is related to the long gaps problem (#4), given how #4 was already closely tied to the sister problem #687 to this one.

Perhaps this one mentioned by Tom Crawford might come in handy: the integers of the interval can seen as aligned lily pads, and the congruences can be seen as frogs that jump on this line, each with a distance of jump of $p$ lily pads. As these frogs jump back and forth, we want each lily pad to be stepped on at least twice by the frogs.

OK, that works for me. I'll keep the previous message unedited with the "ducks" metaphor, but will use the "frogs" metaphor instead for subsequent comments. (An amusing coincidence of notation with Dyson's famous "birds and frogs" metaphor, though.) One small comment: of the $n\log\log z / \log n$ lily pads remaining above the pond surface, actually most of them (all but $O(n/\log n)$) will already have been stepped on by one frog already, and only need one more frog to sink; so in fact just having the $p$-frog for $n/z < p \leq n$ step on about $n/p(\log n/p)$ of the remaining pads would come very close (within a factor of $1-o(1)$) of sinking all the pads (rather than a factor of $2$ as I previously thought), assuming optimistically that each frog hops on an essentially disjoint set of pads. So only a little bit of improvement over this type of strategy may be needed to close the argument.

This is a really minor comment explaining why random sieving seems not to help. The one very minor advantage potentially of using the initial primes as a random sieving step is that there seem to be less issues of $z$-smoothness to think about. (I will also ignore the use of a $W$ trick; it will be required for $W\ll z$ when working through the problem.) Fix $z \le \log n$ for now and consider setting for all primes between $[z,n/z]$ the residue class to be $0 \pmod p$. There are two categories of number left. (1) Numbers which have not been touched at all. These are only the $z$ smooth numbers and primes larger than $n/z$; the second term is dominant. This gives $n/\log n$ integers which will be handled at the end. (2) There are numbers which are only touched exactly once. These integers are precisely of the form $p q$ where $p$ is a prime in $[z,n/z]$ and $q$ is $z$-smooth. The dominant term (from my computations) is when $p$ is near maximal so there are $\approx n\log(z)/\log(n)$ integers remaining. (In fact if one runs through the numerics one can really only pay attention to $pq$ with $p$ prime and $q\le z^{A}$ with $A\to \infty$ arbitrarily slowly at the end.) We now run the random sieve using primes less than $z$. We will try and be "efficient" in the end with primes of the form between $n/z$ and $n/2$; the primes between $n/2$ and $n$ will be used to handle stragglers. This leaves us with $O(n/(\log n \log z))$ integers in the first category and therefore reserving a vanishing small portion of primes in the final category we can handle these integers. For the final category, we have a $\lesssim n/\log n$ integers which survive this sieving so we are back at the "constant" factor off regime in the above post. We now consider using primes between $n/z$ and $n/(2z)$; due to the random sieving we only need to control the integers coming from (2). Initially for each such prime a given shift would have given us $z$ chances to hit the integers; however the random sieving gives us a "random penalty" factor of $\log z$ and so we really need to detect more than $\log z$ primes among the $z$ so the small--gaps technology is unsuitable (this is exactly what Terry says above). For the linear equations in primes technology, it is basically sufficient to try and use primes between $n/z$ and $n/(2z)$ to handle the $pq$ products in (2) with $p\in [n/z^{A},n/z]$. This amounts to solving $p_1 = (\epsilon z) q + t$, $p_2 = (\epsilon z + 1) q + t$,... $p_{\ell} = zq + t$ with $t$ an integer, $q$ is a prime between $n/z$ and $n/(2z)$ and $p_i$ has a prime factor between $n/z^A$ and $n/z$. The set $p_i$ formed by taking products can be proven to be Gowers uniform (up to major arc issues which would be corrected by a $W$-trick) and so the result should follow. (It is also rather difficult to make this quantitative; this requires uniformity in the $k$-aspect of various $U^{k}$-Gowers norms which have not been worked out in any detail.)

I think there are a few more contributions to (1), one coming from prime powers (but this is negligible), and another coming from numbers with a prime factor in $[n/z,n]$ but not itself prime. This doesn't change the numerology too much though, one still has about $O(n \log z / \log n)$ remaining targets before the random sieving step, the bulk of which come from those numbers with a prime factor in $[n/z^A, n/z^{1/A}]$. But it seems that we just barely lose with this strategy even with the most optimistic assumptions. Each $p \in [n/2z, n/z]$ can at best touch $O(z)$ of these targets, or $O(z/\log z)$ after the random sieving step (from the upper bound sieve one cannot get around this $\log z$ loss except possibly for a constant factor). Similarly for larger $p$. Summing over $p$ we can only touch $O(n \log z / \log n )$ targets before random sieving, or $O(n / \log n)$ ones after random sieving, so we lose by a constant factor (which grows in $A$, so probably is greater by one). [EDIT: actually the additional terms in (1) that involve a prime factor between $n/z$ and $n/z^{1/A}$ could be more problematic, as they need to be touched twice in order to win, and so the random sieve I think leaves behind $n \log\log z / \log n$ remaining targets rather than $n / \log n$ if I computed correctly.] My calculations seem to suggest that the non-random sieve (in which one touches $0 \pmod{p}$ for all $p \leq n/z$) still has a chance; somehow the fact that the majority of the remaining targets actually lie in one of the sieved out classes $0 \pmod{p}$, $p \leq z$ seems to work slightly on one's favor, as it allows for more of the congruence class mod $p$ for $n/z < p < n/2$ to be usable for touching slightly more targets (according to my calculations, a congruence class mod $p \sim n/z$ could potentially touch $z \log\log z / \log z$ remaining targets, which is slightly above the break-even point of $z / \log z$). As long as we take $z$ to be a large constant, I don't think the dependence on $k$ in the $U^k$ inverse theory etc. will be of relevance for $n$ large enough. The $W$-trick obstructions to uniformity are annoying, though: it seems that the irregularities at small primes are a crucial part of this story and need to be analyzed carefully.
